chunk_summary: This section outlines the triage and initial treatment of youth-onset type 2 diabetes, emphasizing diagnostic steps, safety in hyperglycemia, and HbA1c-based treatment pathways for pediatric patients.
learning_objectives: Understand the initial diagnostic steps for youth-onset type 2 diabetes, including ketone testing and baseline lab evaluations.
Differentiate between type 2 diabetes and other diabetes phenotypes in children using clinical cues.
Apply HbA1c-based treatment pathways for pediatric patients with type 2 diabetes, including metformin and insulin initiation.
Recognize when inpatient management is necessary for pediatric diabetes cases.
key_facts: Youth-onset hyperglycemia should be treated as 'type uncertain' until proven otherwise.
Check capillary glucose and urine or blood ketones at first contact; refer urgently if ketones are positive or DKA/HHS symptoms are present.
Baseline labs include plasma glucose, HbA1c, fasting lipids, liver enzymes (ALT/AST), and urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio.
For HbA1c <8.5% without ketosis, start metformin and lifestyle therapy; for HbA1c ≥8.5% without ketosis, add basal insulin (0.25–0.5 units/kg/day).
In cases of DKA or HHS, initiate both basal and rapid-acting insulin and manage inpatient.
Diagnostic cues include obesity/acanthosis for insulin resistance, family history for MODY, and extreme insulin resistance with low fat/high TG for lipodystrophy.
reference_quote: Treat new pediatric hyperglycemia as 'type uncertain until proven otherwise' and prioritize ketosis safety. At first contact, check capillary glucose and urine or blood ketones.
document_key_topics: Triage and safety in pediatric hyperglycemia
HbA1c-based treatment pathways for youth-onset type 2 diabetes
